flashcards.Back: A data collision that occurs after the first 64 bytes of a frame have been transmitted. It is a classic symptom of a duplex mismatch between two connected network interfaces.
A condition where one side of a link is set to full-duplex while the other is set to half-duplex. This leads to poor performance and incrementing late collision counters.
A frame that fails the Frame Check Sequence (FCS) calculation. This indicates corruption during transmission, often due to bad cabling or electromagnetic interference.
An Ethernet frame that is smaller than the minimum legal size of 64 bytes. These are typically caused by collisions on half-duplex segments.
An Ethernet frame that exceeds the maximum supported transmission unit (MTU), typically 1518 bytes. They can be caused by faulty hardware or mismatched MTU settings.
A composite counter on a Cisco interface that tracks the total number of errors received, including runts, giants, CRC errors, and ignored frames.
A statistic that tracks the number of frames that the interface failed to transmit successfully due to internal issues like buffer overflows.
A type of input error where a received frame has a non-integer number of octets and an invalid CRC. This often points to physical layer noise or bad transceivers.
A counter that increments when an interface receives a packet but lacks sufficient buffer space to process it, usually due to high system load.
An input error occurring when the switch's internal hardware cannot move data from the interface buffer to memory fast enough, resulting in data loss.
A condition where two connected interfaces are configured with different static bit rates (e.g., 100 Mbps vs 1000 Mbps). This results in a total loss of link (down/down).
A physical interface that repeatedly transitions between the UP and DOWN states. Common causes include loose cables, faulty transceivers, or EMI.
An error that increments when a device has no internal memory available to store a received frame, causing the frame to be dropped immediately.
A counter on half-duplex ports that increments when an interface must wait to transmit because the medium is already busy. High counts indicate network congestion.
An error occurring when an interface loses the physical carrier signal while attempting to transmit. This points to a disconnected cable or hardware failure.
A counter indicating how many times an interface has been completely reinitialized. Frequent resets are a sign of serious hardware or driver issues.
An error detected when a device transmits data for longer than the maximum allowed frame time. This is typically caused by a faulty network interface card.
Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. The protocol used by half-duplex Ethernet to manage how devices share the medium and recover from collisions.
A specialized signal sent by an Ethernet device when it detects a collision. it ensures that all other devices on the segment recognize the collision and stop transmitting.
A physical network segment where data packets can collide. Modern switches eliminate these by providing dedicated bandwidth to every port.
A Cisco port state where the interface is automatically shut down by the system due to a security violation or excessive physical layer errors.
A Cisco interface status indicating that no physical link is detected. Possible causes include a missing cable, bad cable, or wrong pinouts.
Damage to fiber-optic cabling caused by bending it beyond its allowed radius, which leads to signal attenuation and transmission errors.
A collision that occurs on the local segment before the frame has fully left the interface. These are expected and normal in half-duplex environments.
A collision that occurs further down the network segment. The transmitting device only knows about it if it receives a jam signal from another node.
A specific type of input error often related to internal hardware timings or buffer synchronization issues on a network interface.
A legacy interface counter that increments when a device transmits a frame that is larger than the maximum allowed size, similar to a giant.
A counter that increments when an interface receives a frame containing a protocol ID that the local operating system does not recognize.
A mechanism where a device intentionally slows down the reception or transmission rate on an interface to prevent CPU or buffer exhaustion.
A packet loss event that occurs when an interface's input or output queue is completely full and cannot accept any more traffic.
